items.note: Literally 'hard-headed'. Malo je tvrdoglav, ali dobar. (A bit stubborn, but good.)

items.en: hard-working / lazy
items.note: vrijedan also means 'valuable' — context decides. Feminine: vrijedna, lijena.

items.en: shy
items.note: Kao dijete bio sam jako sramežljiv. (As a child I was very shy.)

items.en: open and sociable
items.note: The classic pair for describing extroverts.

items.hr: Moja žena je vesela i puna energije.
items.en: My wife is cheerful and full of energy.
items.note: pun + genitive: puna energije = full of energy.

items.hr: Na prvi pogled djeluje ozbiljno, ali je zapravo jako duhovit.
items.en: At first glance he seems serious, but he's actually very funny.
items.note: djelovati + adverb = to come across as; zapravo = actually. A ready-made two-part description.

activities.title: Describe them correctly
activities.intro: Looks and character with full adjective agreement — gender, number and case, every time.

questions.prompt: Moja sestra je ______. ('tall')
questions.explanation: sestra is feminine, so the adjective takes -a: Moja sestra je visoka.

questions.prompt: Complete with the accusative: Ima ______ smeđu kosu. (kratak)
questions.explanation: kosa is feminine and the object of imati, so the whole phrase goes accusative: kratku smeđu kosu.

questions.prompt: Marko nas uvijek nasmijava. On je jako ______.
questions.explanation: Someone who always makes you laugh (nasmijava) is duhovit — witty, funny.

questions.prompt: Complete the character description: On je jako ______ — nikad se ne ljuti i mirno čeka. ('patient')
questions.explanation: strpljiv = patient. Someone who never gets angry and waits calmly is strpljiv.

questions.prompt: Put the words in order: 'She has long blond hair and blue eyes.'
questions.answer: Ima dugu plavu kosu i plave oči
questions.explanation: Accusative agreement twice over: dugu plavu kosu (f. sg.) and plave oči (pl.).

questions.prompt: What is the opposite of 'vrijedan' (hard-working)?
questions.explanation: vrijedan (hard-working) ↔ lijen (lazy). Learn character adjectives in opposite pairs.

questions.prompt: Complete: Moj susjed je ______ — nikad ne želi promijeniti mišljenje. ('stubborn')
questions.explanation: tvrdoglav (hard-headed) = stubborn — someone who never wants to change their mind.

questions.prompt: 'Na prvi pogled djeluje ozbiljno, ali je zapravo jako duhovit.' What does this mean?
options: He seems serious at first, but is actually very funny.
He is serious and never jokes.
He looked at me seriously for the first time.
He acts funny but is actually serious.
questions.answer: He seems serious at first, but is actually very funny.
questions.explanation: na prvi pogled = at first glance; djelovati = to come across as; zapravo = actually.

days.title: Comparative: stariji, mlađi, ljepši
days.objective: Form comparatives with -iji, -ji and -ši, including the irregulars.
days.paretoFocus: star → stariji, mlad → mlađi, lijep → ljepši, plus irregular dobar → bolji, velik → veći, malen → manji. Comparison powers opinions, shopping and every 'which is better' talk.
drills: Do Unit 42 of A2.hr.
Study Grammar tab: A2 · comparative and superlative.
Form comparatives of 10 adjectives across all three suffixes.
Compare 4 pairs of people/places with 'nego' and 'od'.

items: Give the irregulars: dobar, loš, velik, malen.
Clear your due words in the Words tab.
Compare two cities in 2 sentences.

activities.title: Stariji, mlađi, ljepši — the comparative
activities.intro: Three suffixes cover every Croatian comparative. The default is -iji (stariji); short adjectives take -ji, which fuses with the final consonant (mlad → mlađi); and exactly three adjectives take -ši. Add four irregulars and you can compare anything. 'Than' is od + genitive or nego.

items.en: old → older
items.note: -iji is the default suffix and your safest guess: stariji, noviji, jeftiniji.

items.en: smart → smarter
items.note: The fleeting a drops before -iji: pametan → pametn-iji.

items.en: young → younger
items.note: -ji fuses with the consonant (iotation): d + j → đ.

items.en: strong → stronger
items.note: k + j → č. Likewise: drag → draži (g + j → ž).

items.en: expensive → more expensive
items.note: p + j → plj. Same with b, m, v: grub → grublji.

items.en: quiet → quieter
items.note: h + j → š: tiši, suši.

items.en: beautiful → more beautiful
items.note: One of only THREE adjectives in -ši. Watch the spelling: ije shortens to je.

items.en: easy → easier, soft → softer
items.note: The other two -ši adjectives. That's the complete list: ljepši, lakši, mekši.

items.en: good → better
items.note: Irregular no. 1 — memorize all four as a set.

items.en: bad → worse
items.note: Irregular no. 2. Not 'lošiji' in standard Croatian.

items.en: big → bigger, small → smaller
items.note: Irregulars no. 3 and 4. The full set: bolji, gori, veći, manji.

items.en: Ivan is older than Marko.
items.note: od + genitive is the most common 'than'.

items.en: Ivan is older than Marko.
items.note: nego + nominative — same meaning, needed before clauses: stariji nego što misliš.

items.hr: Moj stan je manji od tvoga, ali jeftiniji.
items.en: My flat is smaller than yours, but cheaper.
items.note: Two comparatives in one natural sentence — your model for today's speaking drill.

activities.title: Three suffixes and four irregulars
activities.intro: Form comparatives across -iji, -ji and -ši, catch the irregulars, and choose between od and nego.

questions.prompt: What is the comparative of 'star' (old)?
questions.explanation: The default suffix -iji: star → stariji. najstariji is the superlative.

questions.prompt: Form the comparative: mlad → ______
questions.explanation: -ji fuses with the final consonant: d + j → đ, so mlad → mlađi.

questions.prompt: What is the comparative of 'dobar' (good)?
questions.explanation: dobar → bolji is irregular (suppletive) — one of the four you must memorize: bolji, gori, veći, manji.

questions.prompt: Form the comparative: lijep → ______
questions.explanation: lijep is one of only three -ši adjectives, and ije shortens to je: ljepši.

questions.prompt: Form the comparative: loš → ______
questions.explanation: loš → gori is irregular. In standard Croatian it is gori, not 'lošiji'.

questions.prompt: What is the comparative of 'velik' (big)?
questions.explanation: velik → veći, another of the four irregulars. najveći is the superlative.

questions.prompt: Complete with the right 'than': Moj brat je stariji ______ mene.
questions.explanation: od + genitive (mene): stariji od mene = older than me. With nego you'd say: stariji nego ja.

questions.prompt: Complete with 'nego': Ova kava je skuplja ______ ona.
questions.explanation: Before a nominative (ona), use nego: skuplja nego ona. With od it would be genitive: skuplja od one.

questions.prompt: Put the words in order: 'Zagreb is bigger than Split.'
questions.explanation: Comparative + od + genitive: veći od Splita. (Split → Splita in the genitive.)

days.title: Superlative & declining comparisons
days.objective: Form naj- superlatives and decline comparatives like adjectives.
days.paretoFocus: Comparatives decline too ('sa starijim bratom', 'najboljeg prijatelja') — knowing this keeps your case system intact when opinions get superlative.
drills: Do Unit 43 of A2.hr.
Form superlatives: najstariji, najljepši, najbolji.
Decline 'moj najbolji prijatelj' through N, A, D, L.
Answer: Tko je najduhovitija osoba koju znaš? Zašto?

items: Say 'with my older brother' (sa starijim bratom).
Clear your due words in the Words tab.
Recall the three comparative suffixes with one example each.

activities.title: Naj- and the declining comparative
activities.intro: The superlative is mechanical: naj- + comparative, always (najstariji, najljepši, najbolji). The real news today is that comparatives and superlatives decline like definite adjectives — so it's sa starijim bratom and vidio sam najboljeg prijatelja. Your case system stays on even when opinions get superlative.

items.en: the naj- rule
items.note: No exceptions: stariji → najstariji, ljepši → najljepši, bolji → najbolji.

items.en: best friend
items.note: The phrase you'll use most. Feminine: najbolja prijateljica.

items.en: the worst day of the week
items.note: loš → gori → najgori. u tjednu = in the week.

items.hr: Ovo je najljepši grad na svijetu.
items.en: This is the most beautiful city in the world.
items.note: na svijetu = in the world — the classic superlative frame.

items.en: older: N, G/A, D/L, I
items.note: Comparatives decline like definite adjectives — the case endings you already know.

items.en: with (my) older brother
items.note: Instrumental after s/sa: sa starijim bratom. The comparative carries the case.

items.en: I saw my best friend.
items.note: Animate accusative = genitive form: najboljeg prijatelja.

items.en: I'm writing a message to my best friend.
items.note: Dative: najboljem prijatelju.

items.en: We talked about the younger sister.
items.note: Locative feminine: o mlađoj sestri — comparatives take the same -oj as other adjectives.

items.hr: jedan od najboljih restorana u gradu
items.en: one of the best restaurants in town
items.note: jedan od + genitive plural: najboljih restorana. A high-frequency review-speak pattern.

items.hr: Tko je najduhovitija osoba koju znaš?
items.en: Who is the funniest person you know?
items.note: Superlative + relative koju (f. acc.). Today's speaking prompt — answer it aloud.

items.en: The biggest change in my life…
items.note: velik → veći → najveći; feminine najveća. A frame you'll need tomorrow.

activities.title: Superlatives through the cases
activities.intro: Form naj- superlatives, then push comparatives and superlatives through the accusative, dative, instrumental and genitive.

questions.prompt: What is the superlative of 'dobar' (good)?
questions.explanation: naj- + the comparative bolji = najbolji. The superlative is always built on the comparative.

questions.prompt: Complete: Ovo je ______ grad na svijetu. (lijep, superlative)
questions.explanation: lijep → ljepši → najljepši: Ovo je najljepši grad na svijetu. (This is the most beautiful city in the world.)

questions.prompt: How do you say 'with my older brother'?
questions.explanation: Comparatives decline: instrumental masculine is starijim, matching bratom — sa starijim bratom.

questions.prompt: Complete the animate accusative: Vidio sam svog ______ prijatelja. (najbolji)
questions.explanation: Animate masculine accusative = genitive form: najboljeg (najboljega) prijatelja.

questions.prompt: Put the words in order: 'Who is the funniest person you know?'
questions.answer: Tko je najduhovitija osoba koju znaš
questions.explanation: Superlative agreeing with osoba (f.): najduhovitija; the relative pronoun koju is feminine accusative.

questions.prompt: What is the superlative of 'loš' (bad)?
questions.explanation: loš → gori → najgori. The irregular comparative carries into the superlative.

questions.prompt: Complete the dative: Pišem poruku ______ prijatelju. (najbolji)
questions.explanation: Dative masculine: najboljem prijatelju — the superlative takes the same case ending as any adjective.

questions.prompt: Ovo je jedan od ______ restorana u gradu. ('one of the best')
questions.explanation: jedan od + genitive plural: jedan od najboljih restorana u gradu.

days.title: Week 15 review + speaking: then vs now
days.objective: Compare your life now with five years ago, live.
days.paretoFocus: 'Then vs now' forces comparatives, past aspect and future plans into one conversation — a complete workout of the phase so far.
drills: 15-minute conversation comparing your life then and now (tutor, partner or spouse).
Use 6 comparatives and 2 superlatives, declined correctly.
Re-do the weakest exercise from Units 41-43.
Note corrections and feed new words to the Words tab.

items: Clear your due words in the Words tab.
Re-say your 3 best comparison sentences.
Recall the irregular comparatives cold.

activities.title: Then vs now: frames for comparing your life
activities.intro: Today you talk about your life now versus five years ago — a conversation that forces comparatives, past-tense aspect and future plans into one workout. These frames give you the skeleton; your real life fills it in. Aim for six comparatives and two superlatives, declined correctly.

items.en: five years ago
items.note: prije + genitive: prije pet godina, prije mjesec dana.

items.hr: Tada sam živio u Americi, a sada živim u Hrvatskoj.
items.en: Back then I lived in America, and now I live in Croatia.
items.note: tada vs sada — the axis of the whole conversation.

items.hr: Prije nisam govorio ni riječ hrvatskoga.
items.en: Before, I didn't speak a word of Croatian.
items.note: ni riječ + genitive = not a (single) word of.

items.hr: Moj hrvatski je sada puno bolji nego prije.
items.en: My Croatian is now much better than before.
items.note: puno + comparative = much better; nego prije = than before.

items.hr: Život mi je mirniji nego prije.
items.en: My life is calmer than before.
items.note: Dative mi = 'my' with states: Život mi je… (miran → mirniji).

items.hr: Posao mi je zanimljiviji, ali su dani kraći.
items.en: My job is more interesting, but the days are shorter.
items.note: kratak → kraći (t + j → ć). Balance a plus with a minus — it sounds natural.

items.hr: Tada sam bio mlađi, ali sam sada sretniji.
items.en: I was younger then, but I'm happier now.
items.note: sretan → sretniji; the fleeting a drops. Masculine participles: bio.

items.hr: Najveća promjena je to što sam se oženio.
items.en: The biggest change is that I got married.
items.note: to što = the fact that; oženiti se = to get married (said of a man).

items.hr: Nekad sam izlazio svaki vikend, a sada ostajem doma.
items.en: I used to go out every weekend, and now I stay at home.
items.note: nekad + imperfective (izlazio) for old habits.

items.hr: S vremenom je sve postalo lakše.
items.en: With time, everything became easier.
items.note: postati + comparative (neuter lakše): things 'became easier'.

items.hr: Sljedeće godine planiramo kupiti veći stan.
items.en: Next year we're planning to buy a bigger flat.
items.note: Temporal genitive sljedeće godine + a declined comparative: veći stan.

items.hr: U budućnosti želim tečno govoriti hrvatski.
items.en: In the future I want to speak Croatian fluently.
items.note: u budućnosti = in the future; tečno = fluently. End the conversation looking forward.

activities.title: Then, now, and the grammar between
activities.intro: The week 15 sweep: comparatives and superlatives from Units 41-43, past habits vs events, and the frames for tonight's conversation.

questions.prompt: Complete (male speaker): Prije pet godina ______ sam u Americi. (živjeti)
questions.explanation: Masculine singular participle of živjeti: živio — Prije pet godina živio sam u Americi.

questions.prompt: Moj hrvatski je sada puno ______ nego prije.
questions.explanation: Comparing with nego prije needs the comparative in the nominative: hrvatski je bolji.

questions.prompt: Complete: Tada sam bio ______, ali sam sada sretniji. (mlad, comparative)
questions.explanation: mlad → mlađi (d + j → đ): I was younger then, but I'm happier now.

questions.prompt: Put the words in order: 'My life is calmer than before.'
questions.answer: Život mi je mirniji nego prije
questions.explanation: The dative clitic mi ('my') and je sit in second position: Život mi je mirniji nego prije.

questions.prompt: Complete (male speaker): Najveća promjena je to što sam se ______.
questions.explanation: oženiti se = to get married (of a man); the masculine participle is oženio: … što sam se oženio.

questions.prompt: Pick the right aspect for an old habit: Nekad sam ______ svaki vikend.
questions.explanation: A repeated past habit (svaki vikend) takes the imperfective izlazio; izašao is a single completed exit.

questions.prompt: Complete the temporal genitive: Sljedeće ______ planiramo kupiti veći stan.
questions.explanation: Time expressions go straight into the genitive: sljedeće godine = next year.

questions.prompt: Ovo su ______ godine mog života. ('the most beautiful')
questions.explanation: godine is feminine plural, so the superlative agrees: najljepše godine — the most beautiful years.

activities.title: Prije pet godina i danas
activities.intro: The real thing: you and your wife compare your life now with five years ago. Read your lines ('Me') aloud and count your comparatives — you need six, plus two superlatives, all declined correctly.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Zamisli — prije pet godina nismo se ni poznavali. Kakav ti je tada bio život?
lines.en: Imagine — five years ago we didn't even know each other. What was your life like then?

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Tada sam živio u Americi i nisam govorio ni riječ hrvatskoga.
lines.en: Back then I lived in America and didn't speak a word of Croatian.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: A sada? Reci mi tri stvari koje su danas drukčije.
lines.en: And now? Tell me three things that are different today.

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Moj hrvatski je puno bolji, život mi je mirniji, a naš stan je manji nego moj stari.
lines.en: My Croatian is much better, my life is calmer, and our flat is smaller than my old one.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Ha! Ali je ljepši, priznaj.
lines.en: Ha! But it's nicer, admit it.

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Istina, ljepši je i topliji. I kvart je tiši nego moj stari kvart.
lines.en: True, it's nicer and warmer. And the neighbourhood is quieter than my old one.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Jesi li sada sretniji nego tada?
lines.en: Are you happier now than you were then?

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Puno sretniji. Tada sam bio mlađi, ali najveća promjena je to što sam se oženio.
lines.en: Much happier. I was younger then, but the biggest change is that I got married.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Dobar odgovor! A što planiramo za budućnost?
lines.en: Good answer! And what are we planning for the future?

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Sljedeće godine želimo kupiti veći stan. I želim tečno govoriti hrvatski.
lines.en: Next year we want to buy a bigger flat. And I want to speak Croatian fluently.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Govorit ćeš. Pa već sada razgovaramo samo na hrvatskom!
lines.en: You will. We're already talking only in Croatian right now!

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Da — i to je najbolji dokaz da se trud isplati.
lines.en: Yes — and that's the best proof that the effort pays off.

days.title: Comparing adverbs: brže, bolje, najviše
days.objective: Compare how things are done: adverb comparatives and superlatives.
days.paretoFocus: brzo → brže, dobro → bolje, mnogo → više — adverb comparison completes the comparing toolkit and appears constantly in everyday evaluation.
drills: Do Unit 44 of A2.hr.
Study Grammar tab: A2 · comparing adverbs.
Form: brzo/brže/najbrže, dobro/bolje/najbolje, loše/gore/najgore, mnogo/više/najviše.
Make 5 sentences about who does what better/faster/most.

items: Say 'She speaks Croatian better than I do.'
Clear your due words in the Words tab.
Review adjective superlatives.

activities.title: Brže, bolje, najviše — comparing how
activities.intro: Adverbs compare with the neuter comparative form: brzo → brže, dobro → bolje. The irregulars mirror the adjectives you learned this week (bolje, gore, više, manje), and naj- again makes the superlative. Add što + comparative for 'as … as possible' and your comparing toolkit is complete.

items.en: fast → faster → fastest
items.note: The adverb comparative is the neuter comparative form: brže.

items.en: slowly → more slowly → most slowly
items.note: Regular -ije, like the adjective sporiji.

items.en: well → better → best
items.note: Mirrors dobar → bolji. Govorim bolje = I speak better.

items.en: badly → worse → worst
items.note: Mirrors loš → gori. Danas se osjećam gore. (I feel worse today.)

items.en: much → more → most
items.note: više also builds quantity: više posla = more work (+ genitive).

items.en: little → less → least
items.note: manje kave = less coffee. The pair to više.

items.en: often → more often → most often
items.note: st + j → šć in disguise: često → češće.

items.en: early → earlier; late → later
items.note: Everyday planning words: Dođi ranije! Krenimo kasnije!

items.hr: Ona govori hrvatski bolje od mene.
items.en: She speaks Croatian better than me.
items.note: Same 'than' rules as adjectives: od + genitive, or nego ja.

items.en: Please speak more slowly.
items.note: The learner's most useful sentence — deploy it freely.

items.en: as soon as possible / as much as possible
items.note: što + comparative = as … as possible: Nazovi me što prije!

items.en: Most of all, I like travelling in summer.
items.note: najviše + voljeti = the standard 'favourite' frame.

activities.title: Who does it better, faster, most?
activities.intro: Compare how things are done: form the adverb comparatives, catch the irregulars, and use što + comparative like a native.

questions.prompt: What is the comparative of 'dobro' (well)?
questions.explanation: dobro → bolje, mirroring dobar → bolji. bolji is the adjective; the adverb is bolje.

questions.prompt: Form the comparative adverb: brzo → ______
questions.explanation: brzo → brže (z + j → ž): Vlak ide brže od autobusa.

questions.prompt: Moja žena vozi ______ od mene.
questions.explanation: Comparing how she drives needs the adverb: vozi bolje od mene. (bolja would describe her, not the driving.)

questions.prompt: Form the comparative: mnogo → ______
questions.explanation: mnogo → više → najviše. Radim više nego prije. (I work more than before.)

questions.prompt: What is the comparative of 'loše' (badly)?
questions.explanation: loše → gore, mirroring loš → gori: Danas igram gore nego jučer.

questions.prompt: Put the words in order: 'She speaks Croatian better than me.'
questions.answer: Ona govori hrvatski bolje od mene
questions.explanation: Adverb after the object: govori hrvatski bolje, then od + genitive (mene) for 'than me'.

questions.prompt: Complete 'as soon as possible': Nazovi me što ______!
questions.explanation: što + comparative = as … as possible: što prije = as soon as possible.

questions.prompt: 'Most of all, I like travelling in summer.' — ______ volim putovati ljeti.
questions.explanation: najviše + voljeti = to like most of all: Najviše volim putovati ljeti.

days.title: Relationships: veze i prijateljstva
days.objective: Talk about relationships, friendship and family dynamics.
days.paretoFocus: Relationship vocabulary (veza, povjerenje, svađa, podrška) plus opinion frames gives you access to the conversations that actually build friendships.
drills: Do Unit 45 of A2.hr.
Learn 12 relationship words: veza, brak, povjerenje, svađa, pomirenje, podrška…
State 3 opinions: Što je najvažnije u prijateljstvu? (superlatives!)
Describe how you met an important person in your life.

items: Clear your due words in the Words tab.
Give your 'most important in friendship' answer cold.
Review adverb comparison.

activities.title: Veze i prijateljstva — the vocabulary of closeness
activities.intro: Twelve words that unlock real conversations about relationships, plus the opinion frames (superlatives!) you need to say what matters most to you. Read every sentence aloud — tonight's topic is your own life.

items.hr: Naša veza je jaka i iskrena.
items.en: Our relationship is strong and honest.
items.note: veza = relationship (also 'connection' — autobusna veza is a bus connection).

items.en: We have been married for five years.
items.note: brak = marriage; u + locative: u braku. Croatian uses the present for 'have been … for'.

items.en: Trust is the foundation of every relationship.
items.note: povjerenje (n.) = trust; svake veze is the genitive after temelj — 'foundation OF'.

items.en: Not every argument is the end of the world.
items.note: svađa = argument, quarrel; the verb is posvađati se = to have a fight.

items.en: After an argument comes reconciliation.
items.note: pomirenje = reconciliation; pomiriti se = to make up. Poslije + genitive: svađe.

items.en: Thank you for the support.
items.note: podrška = support. After na it goes to the locative and k → c (sibilarization): podršci.

items.hr: Naše prijateljstvo traje već dvadeset godina.
items.en: Our friendship has lasted twenty years already.
items.note: prijateljstvo (n.) = friendship; dvadeset + genitive plural: godina.

items.en: Honesty is more important than anything.
items.note: iskrenost = honesty, a feminine i-declension noun. Comparative + od + genitive: važnija od svega.

items.en: Without respect there is no love.
items.note: poštovanje = respect. Both bez and nema take the genitive: poštovanja, ljubavi.

items.hr: Upoznali smo se na poslu prije deset godina.
items.en: We met at work ten years ago.
items.note: upoznati se = to meet (for the first time). prije + genitive = ago.

items.hr: Zaljubio sam se u nju odmah.
items.en: I fell in love with her immediately.
items.note: zaljubiti se u + accusative. After a preposition use the stressed form nju, never ju.

items.hr: Vjenčali smo se prije tri godine.
items.en: We got married three years ago.
items.note: vjenčati se = to get married; after tri the noun takes the paucal form godine.

items.en: The most important thing in friendship is trust.
items.note: Your opinion frame: neuter superlative najvažnije + je + noun. Swap in podrška, iskrenost, humor…

activities.title: What matters most — vocabulary and opinions
activities.intro: First the words, then the frames: build superlative opinions about friendship and tell the story of how you met someone important.

questions.prompt: What does 'povjerenje' mean?
questions.explanation: povjerenje = trust. Argument is svađa, support is podrška, marriage is brak.

questions.prompt: They had a quarrel last night. Which noun names it?
questions.explanation: svađa = argument, quarrel. pomirenje is what hopefully comes afterwards.

questions.prompt: Hvala ti na ___! (podrška — put it after na)
questions.explanation: na + locative, and k sibilarizes to c before -i: podrška → podršci.

questions.prompt: Najvažnije u prijateljstvu je ___.
questions.explanation: After je the thing you name stays in the nominative: Najvažnije … je povjerenje.

questions.prompt: Zaljubio sam se u ___ čim sam je upoznao. (ona)
questions.explanation: u + accusative, and after a preposition you need the stressed form nju — the clitic ju cannot follow a preposition.

questions.prompt: Say: I think honesty is more important than money.
questions.answer: Mislim da je iskrenost važnija od novca
questions.explanation: Opinion frame: Mislim da + clause; je sits right after da, and the comparison uses od + genitive (novca).

questions.prompt: Povjerenje je ___ u svakoj vezi. (važan — superlative, agree with povjerenje)
questions.explanation: povjerenje is neuter, so the superlative is neuter too: naj + važnije = najvažnije.

questions.prompt: ___ smo se na fakultetu prije deset godina. (upoznati — perfekt, mi)
questions.explanation: The 'how we met' opener: Upoznali smo se… — masculine plural participle upoznali, then the clitics smo se.

questions.prompt: Posvađali smo se, ali smo se brzo ___.
questions.explanation: mi (a mixed couple) takes the masculine plural participle: pomirili. The se is already in the clitic chain (smo se).

days.title: Futur II: kad budem imao…
days.objective: Form futur II (budem + participle) and know where it lives.
days.paretoFocus: Futur II never stands alone — it lives inside kad/ako/dok clauses about the future. Learning the form with its habitat prevents years of confusion.
drills: Do Unit 46 of A2.hr.
Study Grammar tab: A2 · futur II and time/condition clauses.
Learn budem, budeš, bude, budemo, budete, budu + participle.
Build 5 sentences: Kad budem imao vremena, nazvat ću te.

items: Conjugate budem… cold.
Say 'When I have time (in future), I'll call you.'
Clear your due words in the Words tab.

activities.title: Futur II: budem + participle
activities.intro: Futur II never stands alone — it lives inside kad/ako/dok clauses about the future, while the main clause keeps futur I. Learn the six forms of budem and the habitat together.

items.en: I, you, he/she (future auxiliary)
items.note: The perfective present of biti. It only appears in dependent clauses — never as a standalone future.

items.en: we, you (pl.), they (future auxiliary)
items.note: Add the past participle after it: budemo radili, budete čekali, budu učili.

items.hr: Kad budem imao vremena, nazvat ću te.
items.en: When I have time (in the future), I'll call you.
items.note: The classic pattern: futur II (budem imao) in the kad-clause, futur I (nazvat ću) in the main clause.

items.hr: Kad budem imala vremena, doći ću.
items.en: When I have time, I'll come. (a woman speaking)
items.note: The participle agrees with the speaker: imao (m.) vs imala (f.).

items.hr: Ako budeš učio svaki dan, položit ćeš ispit.
items.en: If you study every day, you'll pass the exam.
items.note: ako + futur II for future conditions with imperfective verbs (učiti).

items.hr: Dok budeš spavao, ja ću raditi.
items.en: While you're sleeping, I'll be working.
items.note: dok + futur II = an ongoing future background action.

items.hr: Ako bude padala kiša, ostat ćemo doma.
items.en: If it rains, we'll stay home.
items.note: Weather runs on futur II too: bude padala. Note doma = (at) home, everyday speech.

items.hr: Kad dođem kući, javit ću ti se.
items.en: When I get home, I'll get in touch.
items.note: THE key shortcut: with perfective verbs (doći) the plain present replaces futur II. This is the most common pattern of all.

items.en: If you see Ana, say hi to her.
items.note: Perfective vidjeti → present vidiš in the ako-clause; the main clause can even be an imperative.

items.en: I'll wait until you come.
items.note: dok ne + perfective = 'until'. The ne here is not a real negation.

items.hr: Kad budemo živjeli u Hrvatskoj, sve će biti lakše.
items.en: When we're living in Croatia, everything will be easier.
items.note: Imperfective živjeti needs the full futur II: budemo živjeli.

items.hr: Kad budete u mirovini, putovat ćete.
items.en: When you (pl.) are retired, you will travel.
items.note: With biti itself, bude IS the verb — no participle needed: kad budem u mirovini.

activities.title: Budem-drill: forms and habitat
activities.intro: Conjugate budem cold, then place futur II where it belongs — and switch to the plain present when the verb is perfective.

questions.prompt: The futur II auxiliary for 'ja' is:
questions.explanation: ja budem, ti budeš, on bude, mi budemo, vi budete, oni budu.

questions.prompt: Kad ___ imao vremena, nazvat ću te. (ja)
questions.explanation: First person singular: budem + imao. The main clause keeps futur I (nazvat ću).

questions.prompt: The 'oni' form of the auxiliary is:
questions.explanation: oni budu radili — no j anywhere: budu, not budeju.

questions.prompt: Ako budeš ___ svaki dan, položit ćeš ispit. (učiti — participle, to a man)
questions.explanation: budeš + masculine singular participle: učio. Imperfective učiti takes the full futur II.

questions.prompt: Kad ___ kući, javit ću ti se. (doći — careful, it's perfective!)
questions.explanation: With perfective verbs the plain present replaces futur II in kad-clauses: Kad dođem… 'Budem došao' is heavy and rare; 'ću doći' is flat-out wrong here.

questions.prompt: Say: I'll wait until you come.
questions.explanation: dok ne + perfective present = 'until': Čekat ću dok ne dođeš. The ne is part of the 'until' formula, not a negation.

questions.prompt: Dok ___ spavao, ja ću raditi. (ti)
questions.explanation: Second person singular: budeš spavao — an ongoing future background after dok.

questions.prompt: Which sentence is correct?
options: Kad budem imao vremena, nazvat ću te.
Kad ću imati vremena, nazvat ću te.
Kad budem imati vremena, nazvat ću te.
Kad imao budem vremena, nazvat ću te.
questions.answer: Kad budem imao vremena, nazvat ću te.
questions.explanation: Futur I never enters a kad-clause, and budem takes the participle (imao), not the infinitive.

questions.prompt: Ana kaže: Kad budem ___ vremena, doći ću. (imati — participle for Ana)
questions.explanation: The participle agrees with the speaker: Ana is a woman, so imala, not imao.

days.title: Kad, ako, dok + the future
days.objective: Choose correctly between kad (when), ako (if) and dok (while) in future clauses.
days.paretoFocus: Three little conjunctions structure all future planning: certainty (kad), condition (ako), duration (dok). English speakers must unlearn 'will' inside these clauses.
drills: Do Unit 47 of A2.hr.
Contrast: Kad dođeš… / Ako dođeš… / Dok budeš čekao…
Translate 5 English 'when/if I…' sentences without using futur I in the clause.
Write 3 real plans with a kad/ako/dok clause each.

items: Explain kad vs ako in one sentence.
Clear your due words in the Words tab.
Re-say your 3 real plans.

activities.title: Kad, ako, dok — three little words that plan your future
activities.intro: Certainty (kad), condition (ako), duration (dok). Inside all three: futur II for imperfective verbs, plain present for perfective — and never, ever futur I.

items.en: When you come, we'll have lunch.
items.note: kad = when: you ARE coming, only the time is open.

items.en: If you come, we'll have lunch.
items.note: ako = if: maybe you come, maybe you don't. Same grammar, different certainty.

items.hr: Dok budeš čekao, ja ću kupiti karte.
items.en: While you're waiting, I'll buy the tickets.
items.note: dok = while: a stretch of time. Imperfective čekati → futur II budeš čekao.

items.hr: Čekat ću ovdje dok ne dođeš.
items.en: I'll wait here until you come.
items.note: dok ne + perfective = until. Compare: dok budeš čekao (while) vs dok ne dođeš (until).

items.hr: Kad završim posao, otići ću u teretanu.
items.en: When I finish work, I'll go to the gym.
items.note: Perfective završiti → plain present završim. No 'ću' inside the clause!

items.hr: Ako budem imao slobodan vikend, posjetit ćemo tvoje roditelje.
items.en: If I have a free weekend, we'll visit your parents.
items.note: Imperfective imati → full futur II: budem imao.

items.hr: Dok budem putovao, slušat ću hrvatske podcaste.
items.en: While I'm travelling, I'll listen to Croatian podcasts.
items.note: Two parallel future actions: dok + futur II sets the background.

items.hr: Kad stignem, nazvat ću te. (NE: kad ću stići)
items.en: When I arrive, I'll call you.
items.note: The English trap: 'when I WILL arrive' must lose its will in Croatian. Perfective stići → stignem.

items.hr: Ako bude gužve, doći ćemo kasnije.
items.en: If there's a crowd/traffic, we'll come later.
items.note: bude + genitive (gužve) — the existential 'if there is…' pattern.

items.en: When it's done, let me know.
items.note: bude as the full verb 'to be' — no participle. Main clause here is an imperative.

items.hr: Ako nešto ne budeš razumio, pitaj me.
items.en: If you don't understand something, ask me.
items.note: Negative futur II: ne budeš razumio — ne goes directly before the auxiliary.

activities.title: Choose your conjunction
activities.intro: Kad, ako or dok? Then keep 'will' out of the clause: futur II for imperfectives, plain present for perfectives.

questions.prompt: What does 'ako' mean?
questions.explanation: ako = if (condition), kad = when (certainty), dok = while (duration).

questions.prompt: ___ dođeš, ručat ćemo. (We know you're coming — it's arranged.)
questions.explanation: The visit is certain, only the time is open → kad. Ako would question whether you come at all.

questions.prompt: ___ bude padala kiša, ostat ćemo doma. (We have no idea about the weather.)
questions.explanation: Rain is uncertain → ako. Kad bude padala kiša would mean you're sure it will rain.

questions.prompt: Kad ___ posao, nazvat ću te. (završiti — ja)
questions.explanation: Perfective završiti takes the plain present in the kad-clause: završim — never 'ću završiti' here.

questions.prompt: Translate: 'When I arrive, I'll call you.'
options: Kad ću stići, nazvat ću te.
questions.explanation: Unlearn the English 'will': perfective stići → present stignem inside kad. Ako stignem would mean 'IF I make it'.

questions.prompt: Dok ___ čekao, ja ću kupiti karte. (ti)
questions.explanation: Imperfective čekati needs futur II after dok: dok budeš čekao.

questions.prompt: Say: I'll call you when I have time. (main clause first)
questions.answer: Nazvat ću te kad budem imao vremena
questions.explanation: Main clause futur I with clitics ću te in second position, then kad + futur II: budem imao vremena.

questions.prompt: What does 'Čekat ću dok ne dođeš.' mean?
options: I'll wait while you don't come.
I won't wait for you to come.
I'll wait in case you come.
questions.explanation: dok ne + perfective = 'until'. The ne is part of the formula, not a real negation.

questions.prompt: Dok ne ___ novi posao, ne mogu planirati odmor. (naći — ja)
questions.explanation: 'Until I find a new job' — dok ne + perfective present: nađem (naći → nađem, with đ).

days.title: Talking about your future
days.objective: Speak for two minutes about your future mixing futur I and futur II.
days.paretoFocus: 'O budućnosti' is where this module's grammar becomes identity: your plans, conditions and hopes in fluent sequence.
drills: Do Unit 48 of A2.hr.
Prepare a 2-minute talk: next year, in five years, kad budeš u mirovini.
Include 2 futur II clauses and 1 conditional wish.
Record it, listen, and fix the worst sentence.

items: Re-deliver the talk without notes.
Clear your due words in the Words tab.
Review kad/ako/dok with one example each.

activities.title: O budućnosti — building your two-minute talk
activities.intro: Ten sentences that assemble into a fluent talk about your future: next year, in five years, in retirement. Steal them, swap in your real plans, and note the two futur II clauses and the conditional wish.

items.hr: Sljedeće godine planiram položiti ispit iz hrvatskoga.
items.en: Next year I plan to pass the Croatian exam.
items.note: sljedeće godine — temporal genitive, no preposition. planirati + infinitive.

items.hr: Za pet godina želim živjeti bliže moru.
items.en: In five years' time I want to live closer to the sea.
items.note: za + accusative = 'in X years from now'. bliže + dative: moru.

items.hr: Kad budem imao više slobodnog vremena, putovat ćemo češće.
items.en: When I have more free time, we'll travel more often.
items.note: Futur II clause number one. više + genitive: slobodnog vremena; češće = comparative of često.

items.hr: Ako sve bude u redu, kupit ćemo veći stan.
items.en: If everything is all right, we'll buy a bigger apartment.
items.note: bude as full verb — no participle. veći = comparative of velik.

items.hr: Kad budem u mirovini, svaki ću dan šetati uz more.
items.en: When I'm retired, I'll walk along the sea every day.
items.note: Futur II clause number two. Watch the clitic: svaki ĆU dan — ću in second position.

items.hr: Nadam se da ću jednog dana govoriti hrvatski tečno.
items.en: I hope that one day I'll speak Croatian fluently.
items.note: nadati se da + futur I. jednog dana — temporal genitive again.

items.hr: Volio bih raditi manje i živjeti sporije.
items.en: I would like to work less and live more slowly.
items.note: Your conditional wish: volio bih + infinitive. manje, sporije — comparative adverbs.

items.hr: Prvo ću naći bolji posao, zatim ćemo kupiti stan, a na kraju ćemo se preseliti na more.
items.en: First I'll find a better job, then we'll buy a flat, and in the end we'll move to the coast.
items.note: Sequencing glue: prvo…, zatim…, na kraju… — this is what makes two minutes flow.

items.hr: Vjerojatno ćemo ostati u Zagrebu, ali nikad se ne zna.
items.en: We'll probably stay in Zagreb, but you never know.
items.note: vjerojatno = probably; nikad se ne zna — a set impersonal phrase worth stealing whole.

items.hr: Dok budem učio hrvatski, trebat ću tvoju podršku.
items.en: While I'm learning Croatian, I'll need your support.
items.note: dok + futur II, plus yesterday's podrška in the accusative: tvoju podršku.

activities.title: Future-talk tune-up
activities.intro: Polish the exact pieces of your talk: time frames, the conditional wish, and the futur I / futur II split.

questions.prompt: What does 'za pet godina' mean?
questions.explanation: za + accusative points forward: za pet godina = five years from now. 'Ago' is prije pet godina.

questions.prompt: Kad ___ u mirovini, svaki ću dan šetati uz more. (biti — ja)
questions.explanation: With biti, budem is the whole verb — no participle: kad budem u mirovini.

questions.prompt: ___ bih raditi manje i živjeti sporije. (a man's wish: 'I would love to')
questions.explanation: The conditional wish = participle + bih: Volio bih. Volim bih mixes present and conditional — impossible.

questions.prompt: Nadam se da ___ jednog dana govoriti hrvatski tečno. (ja — futur I clitic)
questions.explanation: Inside a da-clause futur I is fine (this is not kad/ako/dok!): da ću govoriti.

questions.prompt: Say: We'll buy a bigger apartment if everything is all right.
questions.answer: Kupit ćemo veći stan ako sve bude u redu
questions.explanation: Main clause futur I (Kupit ćemo), then ako + bude: the condition keeps the futur II auxiliary, never će biti.

questions.prompt: Which sentence mixes futur I and futur II correctly?
options: Kad budem imao više vremena, putovat ćemo češće.
Kad ću imati više vremena, putovat ćemo češće.
Kad budem imao više vremena, budemo putovali češće.
Kad imati budem više vremena, putovat ćemo češće.
questions.answer: Kad budem imao više vremena, putovat ćemo češće.
questions.explanation: Futur II stays in the kad-clause, futur I in the main clause — never the other way round, and never both futur II.

questions.prompt: Vjerojatno ćemo ostati u Zagrebu, ali nikad se ne ___. (znati)
questions.explanation: The impersonal set phrase: nikad se ne zna = you never know — third person singular with se, no subject.

activities.title: Speaking day: our future, out loud
activities.intro: Deliver your future-talk to your wife at the kitchen table. Act out the script first — including the futur II slip she catches — then improvise with your real plans.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Dobro, pričajmo o budućnosti. Što ćeš raditi sljedeće godine?
lines.en: OK, let's talk about the future. What will you do next year?

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Sljedeće godine planiram položiti ispit iz hrvatskoga i naći bolji posao.
lines.en: Next year I plan to pass the Croatian exam and find a better job.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: A za pet godina? Gdje ćemo živjeti?
lines.en: And in five years? Where will we live?

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Ako sve bude u redu, kupit ćemo veći stan, možda bliže moru.
lines.en: If everything is all right, we'll buy a bigger apartment, maybe closer to the sea.

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: I kad ću imati više vremena, putovat ćemo češće.
lines.en: And when I will have more time, we'll travel more often.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Skoro savršeno! Ali iza 'kad' nema futura prvog — kad BUDEM IMAO više vremena.
lines.en: Almost perfect! But there's no futur I after 'kad' — kad BUDEM IMAO more time.

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Kad budem imao više vremena, putovat ćemo češće. Hvala!
lines.en: When I have more time, we'll travel more often. Thanks!

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Bravo! A što ćeš raditi kad budeš u mirovini?
lines.en: Well done! And what will you do when you're retired?

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Kad budem u mirovini, svaki ću dan šetati uz more i kuhati za tebe.
lines.en: When I'm retired, I'll walk along the sea every day and cook for you.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Odličan plan! A ja ću čitati knjige dok ti budeš kuhao.
lines.en: Excellent plan! And I'll read books while you're cooking.

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Volio bih da jednog dana imamo kuću s pogledom na more.
lines.en: I'd love us to have a house with a sea view one day.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: I ja bih to voljela. Vidiš — o budućnosti već pričaš kao pravi Hrvat!
lines.en: I'd love that too. See — you already talk about the future like a real Croat!

days.title: Tko radi, ne boji se gladi — the world of work
days.objective: Command core work vocabulary: jobs, workplace, applying.
days.paretoFocus: Work is the number-one adult conversation topic. posao, plaća, radno vrijeme, životopis, razgovor za posao — this lexicon pays rent forever.
drills: Do Unit 49 of A2.hr.
Learn 15 work words: tvrtka, ured, plaća, smjena, kolega, šef, životopis, natječaj…
Describe your job (or dream job): duties, hours, what you like.
Fill in a mock CV-style form in Croatian: name, education, experience.

items: Clear your due words in the Words tab.
Describe your workday in 4 sentences.
Review futur II once.

activities.title: Tko radi, ne boji se gladi — the world of work
activities.intro: Fourteen work words in real sentences: the office, the pay, the CV, the interview. This lexicon pays rent forever — read each line aloud and swap in your own job.

items.en: I work at a small company.
items.note: tvrtka = company. Locative tvrtki — the tk cluster blocks sibilarization (not tvrtci).

items.hr: Moj ured je na trećem katu.
items.en: My office is on the third floor.
items.note: ured = office; na + locative: na trećem katu.

items.en: Salary isn't everything, but it helps.
items.note: plaća = salary, pay. Don't confuse with plaža (beach)!

items.en: This week I'm working the morning shift.
items.note: smjena = shift: jutarnja, popodnevna, noćna smjena.

items.hr: Moj kolega Ivan uvijek mi pomaže.
items.en: My colleague Ivan always helps me.
items.note: kolega is masculine but declines like žena (a-declension). A female colleague is kolegica.

items.hr: Šef mi je dao slobodan dan.
items.en: The boss gave me a day off.
items.note: šef / šefica = boss. Clitic order: mi je — dative before je.

items.hr: Poslao sam životopis u tri tvrtke.
items.en: I sent my CV to three companies.
items.note: životopis = CV, résumé. After tri: paucal tvrtke.

items.en: I applied for the job opening.
items.note: natječaj = (job) competition, opening; prijaviti se na + accusative.

items.en: Tomorrow I have a job interview.
items.note: razgovor za posao — literally 'conversation for a job'. Your day-117 topic!

items.en: I have flexible working hours.
items.note: radno vrijeme = working hours (singular in Croatian).

items.en: I have five years of experience.
items.note: iskustvo = experience; quantity chains genitives: pet godina (G pl.) iskustva (G sg.).

items.en: My brother is currently unemployed.
items.note: zaposlen = employed, nezaposlen = unemployed. Feminine: zaposlena.

items.hr: Radim od kuće dva dana tjedno.
items.en: I work from home two days a week.
items.note: od kuće = from home; tjedno = per week.

items.hr: U devet imam sastanak sa šefom.
items.en: At nine I have a meeting with the boss.
items.note: sastanak = meeting; sa + instrumental: sa šefom. Fleeting a: sastanak → sastanka.

activities.title: Work-life drill
activities.intro: From plaća to natječaj: check the vocabulary, then the cases it drags along — locative tvrtki, genitive iskustva, paucal tvrtke.

questions.prompt: What does 'plaća' mean?
questions.explanation: plaća = salary. The beach is plaža — one diacritic away from an awkward payday.

questions.prompt: You need to send your CV. In Croatian that's your:
questions.explanation: životopis = CV (life-description). natječaj is the job opening you send it to.

questions.prompt: Radim u maloj ___. (tvrtka)
questions.explanation: Locative of tvrtka is tvrtki — the tk consonant cluster blocks the usual k → c change (compare banka → banci).

questions.prompt: Imam pet godina ___. (iskustvo)
questions.explanation: Genitive singular after the measured phrase: pet godina iskustva — five years OF experience.

questions.prompt: Say: I applied for the job opening.
questions.explanation: Clitic chain in second position: sam before se. prijaviti se na + accusative.

questions.prompt: Šef ___ je dao slobodan dan. (to me — clitic)
questions.explanation: Giving takes the dative clitic mi. me is accusative, mnom instrumental. Order: mi je (dative before je).

questions.prompt: Poslao sam životopis u tri ___. (tvrtka)
questions.explanation: After dva/tri/četiri feminine nouns take the paucal form (looks like genitive singular): tri tvrtke.

questions.prompt: Which sentence is correct? (Ivan is your co-worker.)
questions.explanation: kolega declines like a feminine noun but IS masculine: moj kolega, bolestan. A female colleague would be moja kolegica — bolesna.

days.title: Week 16 review + native listening
days.objective: Consolidate the comparison-and-future block and try real native media.
days.paretoFocus: You're past Unit 49 — strong enough to start mining HRT news for gist. Native input now compounds daily.
drills: 15-minute conversation: work, future plans, comparisons.
Watch one short HRT news item; get the topic and two facts (visuals help — that's allowed at A2).
Re-do the weakest exercise from Units 44-49.
Note 5 new words from the news for the Words tab.

items: Clear your due words in the Words tab.
Summarise the news item in 2 Croatian sentences.
Recall: futur II paradigm + 3 irregular comparatives.

activities.title: News Croatian — your survival kit for HRT
activities.intro: Eight formulas that carry half of every news item, plus two recap lines from the week. Learn these before you press play — the gist strategy is: catch numbers, names and places first.

items.hr: Svaku večer gledamo vijesti u pola osam.
items.en: Every evening we watch the news at half past seven.
items.note: vijesti (f. pl.) = the news. Jedna vijest = one news item.

items.hr: Prema istraživanju, mladi sve kasnije odlaze od roditelja.
items.en: According to a study, young people leave their parents' home later and later.
items.note: prema + dative = according to; a classic news opener.

items.en: The government has decided to increase salaries.
items.note: vlada = government; odlučiti + infinitive — the news verb par excellence.

items.hr: Sve više ljudi radi od kuće.
items.en: More and more people work from home.
items.note: sve + comparative = more and more: sve više, sve manje, sve skuplje.

items.en: A traffic accident occurred.
items.note: doći do + genitive = 'there occurred' — impersonal news formula, always došlo je.

items.en: They're forecasting rain for the weekend.
items.note: najavljivati = to announce/forecast; impersonal 'they' — no subject needed.

items.hr: Cijene su više u odnosu na prošlu godinu.
items.en: Prices are higher compared to last year.
items.note: u odnosu na + accusative = compared to — statistics language.

items.hr: Na autocesti je zastoj od dva kilometra.
items.en: There's a two-kilometre hold-up on the motorway.
items.note: zastoj = traffic jam, hold-up; od + genitive gives the measurement.

items.hr: Kad budem svaki dan gledao vijesti, hrvatski će mi biti sve lakši.
items.en: When I watch the news every day, Croatian will get easier and easier for me.
items.note: Week recap in one line: futur II (budem gledao) + sve + comparative (lakši).

items.hr: dobro → bolje, loše → gore, mnogo → više
items.en: well → better, badly → worse, much → more
items.note: The three irregular comparative adverbs from this block — recite them cold.

activities.title: Ears on: three mini news items
activities.intro: Three short audio news items — listen for the topic and two facts, exactly like with real HRT. Between them, a cold re-test of the week's grammar.

questions.prompt: What does 'vijesti' mean?
questions.explanation: vijesti (f. pl.) = the news; novine = newspaper, vrijeme = weather.

questions.prompt: Listen to the weather report: what happens tomorrow evening?
options: It will snow in most of the country
questions.explanation: …ali navečer stiže kiša s jugozapada = but in the evening rain arrives from the southwest. The thirty degrees is the daytime high.
questions.audioText: Sutra će u većini zemlje biti sunčano i vruće, ali navečer stiže kiša s jugozapada. Najviše temperature bit će oko trideset stupnjeva.

questions.prompt: Sve ___ ljudi radi od kuće. (mnogo — comparative)
questions.explanation: sve + comparative = more and more: mnogo → više. Sve više ljudi (+ genitive plural).

questions.prompt: Listen to the news item: what changes from Monday?
options: Bus tickets in Zagreb become more expensive
Bus tickets in Zagreb become cheaper
Buses in Zagreb stop running on Mondays
A new bus line opens in Zagreb
questions.answer: Bus tickets in Zagreb become more expensive
questions.explanation: …karta će biti skuplja: umjesto jednog eura, jedan euro i pedeset centi — the ticket goes from 1 € to 1.50 €.
questions.audioText: Od ponedjeljka će autobusna karta u Zagrebu biti skuplja. Umjesto jednog eura, karta će koštati jedan euro i pedeset centi.

questions.prompt: Build the news formula: A traffic accident occurred.
questions.explanation: Impersonal došlo je + do + genitive: prometne nesreće. You'll hear this exact frame in every traffic report.

questions.prompt: Govorim hrvatski ___ nego prošle godine. (dobro — comparative)
questions.explanation: Irregular: dobro → bolje. And prošle godine is the temporal genitive you'll drill on day 120.

questions.prompt: Listen to the traffic report: which two facts are true?
options: There was an accident and a two-kilometre queue
The motorway is closed and empty
There was an accident and the road is faster than usual
There is fog and a ten-kilometre queue
questions.answer: There was an accident and a two-kilometre queue
questions.explanation: došlo je do prometne nesreće + zastoj je dug oko dva kilometra: an accident happened and traffic is slower, with a roughly two-kilometre hold-up.
questions.audioText: Na autocesti između Zagreba i Karlovca došlo je do prometne nesreće. Vozi se sporije, a zastoj je dug oko dva kilometra.

questions.prompt: Week recap — which sentence is fully correct?
options: Kad budem imao vremena, gledat ću vijesti svaki dan.
Kad ću imati vremena, gledat ću vijesti svaki dan.
Kad budem imati vremena, gledat ću vijesti svaki dan.
Kad budem imao vremena, budem gledao vijesti svaki dan.
questions.answer: Kad budem imao vremena, gledat ću vijesti svaki dan.
questions.explanation: Futur II (budem + participle) in the kad-clause, futur I in the main clause. No infinitive after budem, no futur I after kad, no futur II standing alone.

activities.title: Speaking day: work, news and plans
activities.intro: Your 15-minute conversation: work, future plans and comparisons — plus a report on the news item you watched. Act out the script, then continue with your real week.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Kako je bilo danas na poslu?
lines.en: How was work today?

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Naporno. Imao sam tri sastanka, ali moj novi kolega mi je puno pomogao.
lines.en: Exhausting. I had three meetings, but my new colleague helped me a lot.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Radiš li sada bolje nego prije?
lines.en: Are you working better now than before?

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Da, radim brže, a i hrvatski govorim puno bolje nego prošle godine.
lines.en: Yes, I work faster, and I also speak Croatian much better than last year.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: A jesi li danas gledao vijesti, kako smo se dogovorili?
lines.en: And did you watch the news today, like we agreed?

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Jesam! Gledao sam kratku vijest na HRT-u i razumio sam temu i dvije činjenice.
lines.en: I did! I watched a short news item on HRT and understood the topic and two facts.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Ozbiljno? Reci mi!
lines.en: Seriously? Tell me!

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Od ponedjeljka će autobusna karta biti skuplja, a najavljuju i kišu za vikend.
lines.en: From Monday the bus ticket will be more expensive, and they're also forecasting rain for the weekend.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Bravo! Sve više razumiješ. A planovi za vikend?
lines.en: Well done! You understand more and more. And plans for the weekend?

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Ako ne bude padala kiša, idemo na Sljeme. Ako bude, ostat ćemo doma i gledati film.
lines.en: If it doesn't rain, we're going to Sljeme. If it does, we'll stay home and watch a film.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Super plan. Vidiš — kad budeš svaki dan gledao vijesti, hrvatski će ti biti sve lakši.
lines.en: Great plan. See — when you watch the news every day, Croatian will get easier and easier for you.

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Nadam se! Svakog dana razumijem malo više.
lines.en: I hope so! Every day I understand a little more.

days.title: Job interview role-play
days.objective: Handle a basic job interview: introduce experience, answer politely.
days.paretoFocus: The interview scenario packs formal register, conditionals, work vocabulary and biography into one high-stakes format. Unit 50 also closes Module 5.
drills: Do Unit 50 of A2.hr — Module 5 complete!
Prepare answers: Recite nam nešto o sebi. Zašto želite ovaj posao?
Role-play the interview aloud, both roles, vi-forms throughout.
Add one polite conditional: Bih li mogao dobiti više informacija o…?

items: Answer 'Recite nam nešto o sebi' in 30 seconds.
Clear your due words in the Words tab.
Note: 50 units down, 30 to go.

activities.title: Razgovor za posao — the interview script
activities.intro: Formal register, vi-forms throughout, one polite conditional. These eleven lines are the skeleton of every Croatian job interview — learn both sides, you'll play both roles.

items.en: Tell us something about yourself.
items.note: The opener. Recite = vi-imperative of reći; o sebi = reflexive 'about yourself'.

items.en: Why do you want this job?
items.note: Prepare a two-sentence answer with jer: …jer volim raditi s ljudima.

items.en: What is your work experience like?
items.note: kakvo agrees with neuter iskustvo. kakav = what kind of.

items.hr: Radio sam pet godina u maloj tvrtki.
items.en: I worked for five years at a small company.
items.note: Your biography frame: participle + broj + godina + u + locative.

items.hr: Moja je najveća prednost to što brzo učim.
items.en: My greatest strength is that I learn quickly.
items.note: prednost = advantage, strength; to što = 'the fact that'. Note je in second position.

items.en: I speak English, and I'm learning Croatian.
items.note: The languages line — a = 'and/while', softer contrast than ali.

items.hr: Bih li mogao dobiti više informacija o plaći?
items.en: Might I get more information about the salary?
items.note: Your polite conditional! bih + li opens the question; više + genitive: informacija; o + locative: plaći.

items.en: When could I start?
items.note: Conditional softens the question — much smoother than Kada počinjem?

items.en: Do you have any questions for us?
items.note: Your cue to use the conditional above. Always have one question ready.

items.hr: Javit ćemo vam se sljedećeg tjedna.
items.en: We'll get back to you next week.
items.note: Clitic chain: ćemo + vam + se. sljedećeg tjedna — temporal genitive.

items.hr: Hvala vam na pozivu. — Hvala vama što ste došli.
items.en: Thank you for the invitation. — Thank YOU for coming.
items.note: hvala + dative + na + locative (pozivu). Stressed vama returns the thanks.

activities.title: Interview drill: vi-forms and polite conditionals
activities.intro: Nail the formal register before the role-play: vi-verbs, the reflexive sebi, the clitic chains, and your polite conditional question.

questions.prompt: What does 'Recite nam nešto o sebi.' mean?
options: Ask us something about the job.
Write us something about your family.
Tell me something about your boss.
questions.explanation: Recite (vi-imperative of reći) + nam (to us) + o sebi (about yourself).

questions.prompt: Recite nam nešto o ___. (about yourself — reflexive)
questions.explanation: o + locative of the reflexive pronoun sebe → o sebi. It points back at whoever is speaking or asked.

questions.prompt: Zašto ___ ovaj posao? (formal 'you')
questions.explanation: Interviews run on vi-forms: želite. želiš is the ti-form — save it for home.

questions.prompt: Radio sam pet ___ u maloj tvrtki. (godina)
questions.explanation: After pet (and all numbers 5+) the noun takes the genitive plural: pet godina.

questions.prompt: Ask politely: Might I get more information about the salary?
questions.answer: Bih li mogao dobiti više informacija o plaći
questions.explanation: The conditional question: Bih + li first, then mogao + infinitive. više + genitive plural (informacija), o + locative (plaći).

questions.prompt: Hvala vam na ___. (poziv)
questions.explanation: hvala na + locative: poziv → pozivu. The person thanked stays in the dative (vam).

questions.prompt: The smoothest way to ask when you could start:
options: Kada ću početi raditi kod vas?
questions.explanation: The conditional bih mogao softens the question to a polite inquiry — exactly the register an interview needs.

questions.prompt: Javit ćemo ___ se sljedećeg tjedna. (to you, formal — clitic)
questions.explanation: Clitic order: auxiliary ćemo → dative vam → se. javiti se + dative = to get in touch with.

activities.title: Role-play: the interview, both roles
activities.intro: Your wife plays the interviewer — vi-forms from both sides until she breaks character at the end. Play it once as written, then swap roles.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Dobar dan, izvolite, sjednite. Recite nam nešto o sebi.
lines.en: Good afternoon, please, have a seat. Tell us something about yourself.

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Dobar dan, hvala vam na pozivu. Radio sam pet godina u maloj tvrtki i imam puno iskustva s klijentima.
lines.en: Good afternoon, thank you for the invitation. I worked for five years at a small company and I have a lot of experience with clients.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Zašto želite ovaj posao?
lines.en: Why do you want this job?

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Želim ovaj posao jer volim raditi s ljudima. Moja je najveća prednost to što brzo učim.
lines.en: I want this job because I love working with people. My greatest strength is that I learn quickly.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Kakvo je vaše radno iskustvo? Što ste točno radili?
lines.en: What is your work experience like? What exactly did you do?

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Vodio sam male projekte i svaki dan surađivao s kolegama iz drugih zemalja.
lines.en: I led small projects and worked every day with colleagues from other countries.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Govorite li strane jezike?
lines.en: Do you speak foreign languages?

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Govorim engleski, a učim hrvatski. Svakog dana vježbam sa svojom ženom.
lines.en: I speak English, and I'm learning Croatian. I practise every day with my wife.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Odlično. Imate li pitanja za nas?
lines.en: Excellent. Do you have any questions for us?

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Da. Bih li mogao dobiti više informacija o plaći i radnom vremenu?
lines.en: Yes. Might I get more information about the salary and the working hours?

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Naravno, sve ćemo vam poslati. Javit ćemo vam se sljedećeg tjedna. Hvala vam što ste došli.
lines.en: Of course, we'll send you everything. We'll get back to you next week. Thank you for coming.

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Hvala vama. Doviđenja!
lines.en: Thank YOU. Goodbye!

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Bravo! Cijeli razgovor na 'vi' i nijedne greške. Posao je tvoj!
lines.en: Bravo! The whole interview in vi-forms and not a single mistake. The job is yours!

days.title: Genitive across the whole phrase (+ čujem)
days.objective: Decline adjectives, demonstratives, possessives and svoj in the genitive; conjugate čuti-type verbs.
days.paretoFocus: The genitive is Croatian's busiest case; extending it across full phrases ('mog novog šefa') completes your declension table. Bonus: čuti → čujem covers piti, čuti and friends.
drills: Do Unit 51 of A2.hr.
Study Grammar tab: A2 · adjective declension (genitive).
Decline 6 phrases: taj novi kolega → tog novog kolege.
Conjugate čuti (čujem…) and use it in 3 sentences.

items: Say 'my new boss's office' with genitive.
Clear your due words in the Words tab.
Review interview answers.

activities.title: The genitive across the whole phrase (+ čujem)
activities.intro: Not just the noun — the adjective, the demonstrative and the possessive all slide into the genitive with it: taj novi kolega → tog novog kolege. Bonus paradigm: čuti → čujem, the -jem class.

items.hr: taj novi kolega → tog novog kolege
items.en: that new colleague → of that new colleague
items.note: Every word declines: taj → tog, novi → novog, kolega → kolege (a-declension masculine!).

items.hr: moj novi šef → mog novog šefa
items.en: my new boss → of my new boss
items.note: moj → mog(a), novi → novog(a), šef → šefa. The longer forms moga/novoga are equally correct.

items.hr: To je ured mog novog šefa.
items.en: That is my new boss's office.
items.note: Your review-block sentence — the whole possessor phrase sits in the genitive after ured.

items.hr: ta mlada žena → te mlade žene
items.en: that young woman → of that young woman
items.note: Feminine genitive singular: everything ends in -e: te mlade žene.

items.hr: ovo veliko selo → ovog velikog sela
items.en: this big village → of this big village
items.note: Neuter patterns with masculine: -og + -og + -a.

items.en: a good friend → of a good friend
items.note: Fleeting a! dobar loses its a before the ending: dobrog, not dobarog.

items.hr: moja starija sestra → moje starije sestre
items.en: my older sister → of my older sister
items.note: Comparatives decline too: starija → starije.

items.en: My new colleague isn't here today.
items.note: nema + genitive expresses absence — and the whole phrase carries the case.

items.en: I work near my own apartment.
items.note: blizu + genitive; svog because the subject (ja) is the owner.

items.hr: čuti: čujem, čuješ, čuje, čujemo, čujete, čuju
items.en: to hear: I hear, you hear, he/she hears, we hear, you (pl.) hear, they hear
items.note: The -jem class: infinitive in -ti, present stem in čuj-.

items.en: to drink: I drink, you drink, he/she drinks…
items.note: Same pattern as čuti: pij- + em. Also: obuti → obujem.

items.en: Talk to you tomorrow! (literally: we hear each other tomorrow)
items.note: The set phrase for ending calls and texts — čuti se = to be in touch.

activities.title: Full-phrase genitive drill
activities.intro: Decline whole phrases, mind the fleeting a and the a-declension kolega, and conjugate čuti cold.

questions.prompt: The genitive of 'taj novi kolega' is:
questions.explanation: Every word declines: taj → tog, novi → novog, and kolega follows the a-declension: kolege.

questions.prompt: čuti — the 'ja' form: ___
questions.explanation: The -jem class: čuj + em = čujem. Same as piti → pijem.

questions.prompt: Oni ___ glazbu iz susjednog stana. (čuti)
questions.explanation: Third person plural of the -jem class ends in -u: čuju (like piju).

questions.prompt: To je ured ___ novog šefa. (moj)
questions.explanation: Genitive masculine of moj: mog(a)/mojeg(a) — all four variants are standard.

questions.prompt: Nema moje starije ___ danas. (sestra)
questions.explanation: nema + genitive; feminine singular: moje starije sestre — the whole phrase in -e.

questions.prompt: The genitive of 'pametan' (clever) is:
questions.explanation: Fleeting a: pametan drops the second a before endings — pametnog, like dobar → dobrog.

questions.prompt: End the phone call: Talk to you tomorrow at nine!
questions.explanation: čuti se = to be in touch. Verb-first sentences put se right after the verb: Čujemo se…

questions.prompt: Ivan se boji ___ šefa. (his own boss)
questions.explanation: bojati se + genitive, and the owner is the subject (Ivan) → svog, not njegovog.

questions.prompt: Translate: 'my new boss's office'
questions.explanation: Possessed noun first, then the whole possessor phrase in the genitive: ured + mog novog šefa. No preposition needed.

days.title: Whose is it? Genitive of possession
days.objective: Use genitiv pripadnosti for ownership and belonging.
days.paretoFocus: 'auto moga brata', 'centar grada' — the possession genitive is how Croatian chains nouns together, replacing a whole English preposition system.
drills: Do Unit 52 of A2.hr.
Study Grammar tab: A2 · uses of the genitive.
Build 8 possession chains: vrata učionice, kraj filma, adresa moje sestre.
Decide when a possessive adjective (bratov) beats a genitive (moga brata) — rule of thumb: adjectives for single named owners.

items: Say 'my brother's car' both ways.
Clear your due words in the Words tab.
Review genitive phrase endings.

activities.title: Genitiv pripadnosti — whose is it?
activities.intro: Croatian chains nouns with the genitive where English says 'of' or uses apostrophe-s: auto moga brata, centar grada. Plus the rule for when a possessive adjective (bratov, Anin) beats the genitive.

items.en: my brother's car
items.note: Possessed thing first, owner in the genitive after it: auto + moga brata.

items.en: the classroom door
items.note: učionica → učionice. Croatian never says 'učionica vrata' — the order is fixed.

items.en: the end of the film
items.note: kraj = end; also početak filma = the beginning of the film.

items.en: my sister's address
items.note: Feminine owner: moja sestra → moje sestre.

items.en: the city centre
items.note: grad → grada. So common it works like one word: idem u centar grada.

items.en: our neighbours' flat
items.note: Genitive plural: naši susjedi → naših susjeda.

items.en: brother's car, Ivan's flat
items.note: Possessive adjective in -ov/-ev for a single, unmodified masculine owner.

items.en: Ana's mobile, sister's flat
items.note: -in for feminine owners: Ana → Anin, sestra → sestrin.

items.hr: auto moga starijeg brata (NE: starijeg bratov auto)
items.en: my older brother's car
items.note: The rule of thumb: as soon as the owner has a modifier (moj, stariji…), the adjective is impossible — genitive only.

items.en: the door of my boss's office
items.note: Genitives chain: vrata + ureda + mog šefa. English needs two 'of's — Croatian just stacks cases.

items.en: the smell of the sea and the sound of the waves
items.note: The explanatory genitive — what something consists of or evokes: mora, valova (G pl. of val).

activities.title: Possession chains
activities.intro: Build ownership both ways — genitive chains and possessive adjectives — and know exactly when each one is allowed.

questions.prompt: What does 'centar grada' mean?
questions.explanation: centar + genitive grada = the centre OF the city.

questions.prompt: Nismo vidjeli kraj ___. (film)
questions.explanation: The 'of' genitive: kraj filma = the end of the film. film → filma.

questions.prompt: vrata ___ (učionica — the classroom door)
questions.explanation: Feminine genitive singular: učionica → učionice. Possessed noun first, owner after.

questions.prompt: Trebam adresu moje ___. (sestra)
questions.explanation: The owner phrase goes to the genitive: moja sestra → moje sestre.

questions.prompt: 'Ana's mobile' using a possessive adjective:
questions.explanation: Feminine owners take -in: Ana → Anin. It then agrees like an adjective: Anin mobitel, Anina torba.

questions.prompt: How do you say 'my older brother's car'?
questions.explanation: The owner carries modifiers (moj, stariji), so the possessive adjective bratov is blocked — only the genitive works.

questions.prompt: Say: That is my older brother's car.
questions.answer: To je auto moga starijeg brata
questions.explanation: To je + possessed noun + whole owner phrase in the genitive: moga starijeg brata.

questions.prompt: When MUST you use the genitive instead of bratov/Anin?
options: When the owner has a modifier (moj, stariji, naš…)
When the owner is a woman
When the owned thing is plural
questions.answer: When the owner has a modifier (moj, stariji, naš…)
questions.explanation: bratov auto is fine for a bare single owner; add any modifier and the genitive takes over: auto moga brata.

questions.prompt: Ne znam adresu ___ novih susjeda. (naš)
questions.explanation: Genitive plural across the phrase: naši novi susjedi → naših novih susjeda.

days.title: Time in the genitive
days.objective: Use vremenski genitiv for when things happen(ed).
days.paretoFocus: prošlog tjedna, svakog jutra, ove godine, jednog dana — the temporal genitive is the default way to timestamp stories, no preposition needed.
drills: Do Unit 53 of A2.hr.
Learn the set: prošlog/ovog/sljedećeg + tjedna/mjeseca; prošle/ove/sljedeće + godine; svakog jutra/dana.
Timestamp 6 sentences about your recent past and near future.
Start a story with 'Jednog dana…' and add 3 sentences.

items: Say 'last week', 'every morning', 'next year' — genitive only.
Clear your due words in the Words tab.
Review genitive of possession.

activities.title: Vremenski genitiv — timestamps without prepositions
activities.intro: prošlog tjedna, svakog jutra, ove godine — the temporal genitive is how Croatian timestamps stories, no preposition needed. Masculine time words take -og, godina takes -e. Learn the set, then start a story with Jednog dana…

items.en: last week
items.note: prošli tjedan in the genitive — no preposition, the case alone does the job.

items.en: this week
items.note: ovaj → ovog + tjedna. Same slot: ovog mjeseca, ovog vikenda.

items.en: next month
items.note: sljedeći → sljedećeg; mjesec → mjeseca.

items.en: last year
items.note: godina is feminine, so the whole phrase takes -e: prošle godine.

items.en: this year
items.note: ova → ove + godine.

items.en: next year
items.note: The full feminine set: prošle / ove / sljedeće + godine.

items.en: every morning
items.note: svaki + neuter jutro → svakog jutra. Also: svake večeri (every evening, f.).

items.en: every day
items.note: svakog dana = temporal genitive; svaki dan (accusative) is equally common — both are correct.

items.en: one day
items.note: Points forward or back: jednog dana = some day / once upon a time. Every story starts here.

items.hr: Jednog dana preselit ćemo se na more.
items.en: One day we'll move to the coast.
items.note: Story-opener + futur I. Continue with three more sentences — that's your drill.

items.hr: Prošlog smo tjedna bili kod tvojih roditelja.
items.en: Last week we were at your parents' place.
items.note: The clitic smo slides into second position — right inside the time phrase. Prošlog tjedna smo bili… is also fine.

items.en: Next month I start a new job.
items.note: The pattern in one line: masculine -og + tjedna/mjeseca/dana; feminine -e + godine/večeri.

activities.title: Timestamp drill
activities.intro: Last week, every morning, next year — genitive only, no prepositions. Watch the gender split: -og for tjedan/mjesec/dan, -e for godina.

questions.prompt: What does 'prošle godine' mean?
questions.explanation: prošli = last/past; prošle godine = last year (feminine genitive).

questions.prompt: 'Last week' with the temporal genitive:
questions.explanation: tjedan is masculine: prošlog tjedna. (Colloquial prošli tjedan exists too, but today you drill the genitive.)

questions.prompt: ___ godine počeo sam učiti hrvatski. (prošli — agree with godina)
questions.explanation: godina is feminine, so the genitive is prošle godine — not prošlog.

questions.prompt: Svakog ___ pijem kavu na balkonu. (jutro)
questions.explanation: Neuter jutro → genitive jutra: svakog jutra = every morning, no preposition.

questions.prompt: 'Next month' is:
questions.explanation: Masculine agreement all the way: sljedećeg (not sljedeće) + mjeseca (G sg. of mjesec).

questions.prompt: Sljedeće ___ putujemo u Dalmaciju. (godina)
questions.explanation: sljedeće godine = next year. Croatian happily uses the present (putujemo) for planned futures.

questions.prompt: Start the story: One day we'll move to the coast.
questions.answer: Jednog dana preselit ćemo se na more
questions.explanation: Temporal genitive opener (Jednog dana), then futur I with the clitics ćemo se after the infinitive stem.

questions.prompt: Which sentence is fully correct?
options: Prošlog smo tjedna bili kod tvojih roditelja.
Smo prošlog tjedna bili kod tvojih roditelja.
Prošlog tjedan smo bili kod tvojih roditelja.
Prošlog smo tjedna bili kod tvojih roditelji.
questions.answer: Prošlog smo tjedna bili kod tvojih roditelja.
questions.explanation: Clitics can't open a sentence (rules out option 2); the time phrase must agree (prošlog TJEDNA); kod takes the genitive (roditelja).

questions.prompt: Jednog ___ imat ćemo kuću s pogledom na more. (dan)
questions.explanation: dan → genitive dana: jednog dana = one day. Your story from day 114 now has its timestamp.

days.title: Genitive of quantity & description
days.objective: Use the explanatory genitive and quantity genitive fluently.
days.paretoFocus: čaša hladne vode, čovjek dobre volje, puno posla — the genitive after quantities and in descriptions rounds out the case's three big uses.
drills: Do Unit 54 of A2.hr.
Build 6 quantity phrases: šalica jake kave, kilogram svježeg sira, malo slobodnog vremena.
Describe 3 people with objasnidbeni genitiv: žena vedrog duha…
Recap all three genitive uses aloud: time, possession, description/quantity.

items: Clear your due words in the Words tab.
Order 'a glass of cold water' correctly.
Name the three genitive uses with one example each.

days.title: Write it: apply for a job
days.objective: Write a short job application and mini-CV in Croatian.
days.paretoFocus: The application letter is formal writing with immediate real-world value — and it audits your genitives, conditionals and vi-forms in one page.
drills: Do Unit 55 of A2.hr.
Write 10 sentences: Javljam se na natječaj… + experience, strengths, availability.
Attach a 6-line CV: education, work history with temporal genitives (prošle godine…).
Read it to your partner and fix what they flag.

items: Read the letter aloud, formal register.
Clear your due words in the Words tab.
Review the genitive uses once more.

days.title: Week 17 speaking day (+ Unit 56)
days.objective: Talk about work and ambitions live, then bank a lighter unit.
days.paretoFocus: Explaining your work in Croatian to a real listener is the strongest retrieval practice this module allows — everything from Unit 49 onward feeds it.
drills: 15-minute conversation: your work, your ideal job, workplace stories.
Do Unit 56 of A2.hr.
Work in 3 temporal genitives and 2 comparatives naturally.
Note corrections; queue unknown words in the Words tab.

items: Clear your due words in the Words tab.
Re-say your 3 best corrected sentences.
Recall: three uses of the genitive.

days.title: Sjećanja — childhood memories
days.objective: Open the memories theme: childhood vocabulary and first recollections.
days.paretoFocus: djetinjstvo, uspomena, sjećati se — memories are the A2 storytelling theme, and everything you know about past aspect finally gets a stage.
drills: Do Unit 57 of A2.hr.
Learn 12 memory words: djetinjstvo, uspomena, sjećati se, odrastati, igračka, susjedstvo…
Answer: Čega se najradije sjećaš iz djetinjstva? (genitive after sjećati se!)
Sketch 5 bullet points of a childhood memory for tomorrow's story.

items: Clear your due words in the Words tab.
Say 'I remember my grandmother's garden.'
Review temporal genitives for storytelling.

days.title: Sequencing a story
days.objective: Chain events with najprije, zatim, potom, kasnije, na kraju.
days.paretoFocus: Sequencers plus temporal genitives are the skeleton of narrative — the exact linking skill the A2 writing descriptors name.
drills: Do Unit 58 of A2.hr.
